coredata.dc:title: Improvement of hydrophilicity and cell attachment of polycaprolactone scaffolds using green synthesized carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Ehtesabi, H.
Massah, F.
coredata.dc:description: Scaffolds obtained from polycaprolactone (PCL) have received extensive explorations due to their biocompatibility and biomechanical features. Nevertheless, weak hydrophilicity limits their application for scaffold-aided tissue engineering. In this work, surface modification of PCL scaffolds by using the carbon dots (CDs) solutions was examined to increasing the hydrophilic property of PCL scaffolds. Mesenchymal cell growth followed up to further investigation and analysis on the biologic functions of the scaffolds. Our findings show an improvement in cell proliferation and attachment to the modified scaffold. The CD-based scaffold possessed surface roughness, pore structure, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility. The procedural situation, structural manufacture, and optimizing the functionality were screened carefully. Thus, this practical technique of PCL modification gives more intelligent biomedical scaffolds and tissue engineering agents with adjustable designs and desirable performances.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Natural precursor
PCL
Surface modification
Wettability
Cell adhesion

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to those who supported them, especially the vice-presidency for research and technology, Shahid Beheshti University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of CDs and application for surface modification of scaffolds.
(a) UV–visible spectrum, (b) Photoluminescence spectrum, (c) Excitation dependent emission (d) DLS diagram, (e) TEM image, (f) Raman spectrum and (g) FTIR spectroscopy of CDs.
SEM images of nanofibrous structures fabricated using electrospinning, (a, b) PCL, 10 and 2 μm, (c, d) CD/PCL, 10 and 2 μm, respectively.
(a) Stability of CDs in scaffolds, (b) MTT assay results.

The fluorescence imaging from DAPI staining of mesenchymal cells cultivated following 24 h on the scaffolds (a) PCL, (b) CD/PCL. (Arrows showed scaffold fibers that contain CDs. They emitted fluorescence due to the excitation at the wavelength used, but as can be seen, they did not interfere with DAPI staining).
The SEM micrographs of cell attachment on scaffolds (a) PCL after 1 day, (b) PCL after 3 days, (c) CD/PCL after 1 day and (d) CD/PCL after 3days.
Comparison of optical characteristics, quantum yield, and application of the CDs derived from different eco-friendly carbon precursors.
The contact angle of PCL and CD/PCL scaffolds.

head.ce:title: Improvement of hydrophilicity and cell attachment of polycaprolactone scaffolds using green synthesized carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Life Sciences and Biotechnology, Shahid Beheshti University G.C., Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scaffolds obtained from polycaprolactone (PCL) have received extensive explorations due to their biocompatibility and biomechanical features. Nevertheless, weak hydrophilicity limits their application for scaffold-aided tissue engineering. In this work, surface modification of PCL scaffolds by using the carbon dots (CDs) solutions was examined to increasing the hydrophilic property of PCL scaffolds. Mesenchymal cell growth followed up to further investigation and analysis on the biologic functions of the scaffolds. Our findings show an improvement in cell proliferation and attachment to the modified scaffold. The CD-based scaffold possessed surface roughness, pore structure, hydrophilicity, and biocompatibility. The procedural situation, structural manufacture, and optimizing the functionality were screened carefully. Thus, this practical technique of PCL modification gives more intelligent biomedical scaffolds and tissue engineering agents with adjustable designs and desirable performances.

ce:section.ce:para: In novel tissue engineering, damaged tissues can be replaced or regenerated with the help of 3-D scaffolds. Biocompatibility, degradability, appropriate porosity, and physicochemical activity are the main characteristic properties of a competence scaffold [1,2]. Simultaneous influence of these parameters should provide a spatial environment for cell growth and a physiochemical substrate for cell attachment that results in proliferation, differentiation, active integration to the host tissue, and finally regenerate the injured tissue [3,4]. Attachment of alternative cells to the scaffold is the primary step toward tissue engineering, but this complicated process suffers from low hydrophilicity. Various surface modification strategies were examined to solve this obstacle, including laser irradiation, chemical treatment, and protein [5,6]. Among the wide variety of materials used in scaffold fabrication, polycaprolactone (PCL) shows remarkable biocompatibility, slow degradation rate, and important mechanical properties, but hydrophobicity is a great obstacle to its competence in bioengineering applications [7]. Hence, a large number of materials used modulation of the hydrophobicity, mechanical property, biocompatibility, and cellular response of PCL scaffolds [8,9]. CDs, as carbon-based nanomaterials are powerful candidates to enhance PCL-based scaffold hydrophilicity. In addition, CD’s potential in biological applications relies on their excellent photostability, biocompatibility, cellular uptake, and less toxicity [10–12]. A variety of precursors such as citric acid (CA), Phenylenediamine (PDA), thiourea, and ethylamine are used for the preparation of CDs through chemical oxidation, electrochemical carbonization, laser ablation, microwave irradiation, and hydrothermal treatment methods [13]. Recently, naturally green, eco-friendly, and cheap precursors have gained advantages to utilize in the CDs production [14–18]. Due to their conductivity, the incorporation of CDs in polymer structures decreases the agglomeration of whole particles, which modifies the physicochemical features of polymeric and nanofiber scaffolds [19,20]. Electrospinning is an adjustable approach of nanofiber scaffold preparation in tissue engineering that is available for simple management of desired scaffold parameters, including hydrophilicity, conductivity, stretchability, degradability, and other mechanical and chemical properties [21,22]. Furthermore, a high pore structure can be obtained with an electrospinning technique that is suitable for controllable loading and releasing of nutrient material [23,24]. The size, porosity, and other mentioned properties of nanofibers are manipulated by adjusting the electric field, polymer concentration, solution viscosity, spinneret-to-collector distance, and humidity [25–27].

In this study, we apply CDs for physicochemical modification of PCL scaffolds to improve cell attachment. The hydrothermal approach was selected for the green synthesis of CDs from seeds of Nigella sativa. The CDs exhibited blue fluorescence with maximum emission at 430 nm. SEM, contact angle measurements, and tensile strength test characterized the electrospun PCL/CDs scaffold. The bioactive potential of the scaffold was ensued by the MTT test and DAPI staining. The diagram of the prepared CDs and their utilizations for modifying the surface of the scaffold is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Nigella Sativa was purchased from the store in Tehran, Iran. Mesenchymal cells were purchased from the Cell Bank of the Cell Technology Research Center in Iran. Polycaprolactone (PCL) with a molecular mass of 80,000 g/mol, dimethylformamide (DMF), chloroform, dimethylthiazol-2-y1–2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI), paraformaldehyde 4%, glutaraldehyde 2.5%, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), phosphate buffer saline (PBS), Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) containing 5% FBS (fetal bovine serum), ethanol, trypsin enzyme, and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Deionized water was used in all experiments.

CDs were synthesized through a one-step hydrothermal method from Nigella Sativa. Nigella Sativa was purchased from the store and was completely powdered, and 2 g of powder was poured into a stainless-steel autoclave with 20 ml of deionized water and was then placed in the oven at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling the autoclave at room temperature, the resulting solution was centrifuged for 20 min at 4000 rpm. The supernatant was filtered through a PTFE syringe filter with a pore size of 0.22 μm. Resultant CDs are stored in the dark for future using.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a LAMBDA 950 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, America) and a fluorescence spectrometer LS-45 (PerkinElmer, America), respectively. A CM300 transmission electron microscope at 200 kV was used to confirm the morphological characteristics of the fabricated CDs (Philips, Netherlands). For preparing the samples, the CDs solution was subjected to ultrasonic waves (Misonix – S3000, USA) for 20 min and then carefully placed on a carbon-coated microscopic copper mesh. The sample is then placed in a place away from dust for a while to be dried, after which imaging is performed. Dynamic light scattering was studied by Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS to determine the CD size distribution in automatic mode with 90° scattering angle and He–Ne laser light source with a maximum intensity of 10 mW and a wavelength of 632.8 nm. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) in the range 400–4000 cm−1, and the elemental analyzer was used for characterization (Elementar, Germany). The Raman spectrum was obtained using Takram Raman microscope with ND: YAG laser and a wavelength of 532 nm in the range 100–4600 cm−1 (Teksan, Iran).

To calculate the QY, CDs solution (dilution 1/50) with quinine sulfate (reference material) was used. The refractive index of sample and reference material is approximately 1.33, and the QY of the reference material, when dissolved in 1 M H2SO4 solution, is approximately 54.6%. The following formula is used to calculate the relative QY:
(1)
φ
=
φ R
×
I n t
I n
t R
1
−
10
−
A R
1
−
10
− A
n 2
n R 2
ɸ is a QY, Int is the area under the emission curve in terms of wavelength, A is the absorption or density of light at the excitation wavelength, and n is the refractive index of the solution. Subtitle R specifies the relative value of the standard material.

Two types of PCL scaffolds were obtained through the electrospinning method (Full Option Lab2 ESII-II, Nanoazma, Iran). In both procedures, 0.6 g of PCL was dissolved in 4 ml of chloroform beaker. Then, 1 ml of DMF was added to each solution. In the next step, 0.1 g of CDs was added to one of the beakers, and both beakers were placed on a magnetic stirrer for 20 h to obtain homogeneous solutions. Eventually, the solutions were placed in an electrospinning device. The flow rate of the polymer solution was fixed at 0.4 ml/h. The high voltage of 19 kV was employed to the needle tip to maintain consistency in the electrospinning process at a distance of 20 cm between the tip and the collector. An aluminum sheet was used to collect the PCL fibers thereon with a 500 rpm rotation speed. Consequently, two types of scaffolds were obtained: 1) PCL scaffold and 2) CD/PCL scaffold.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN MIRA3, Czech Republic, maximum voltage of 15kv) has been used to show the shape and diameters of PCL and CD/PCL scaffolds. Scaffolds pieces with the size of 1 cm × 4 cm were provided for the tensile strength test (Universal traction tester, Interface Company, USA). Test speed is from 5 mm/min to 500 mm/min. Maximum Elongation is 900 mm, and Max Force is 100 kn Min force: 5 kn.

The drop shape method was used to evaluate the wettability of scaffolds (Prime Ruler). Briefly, during 10 s, 10 μl of distilled water seep on the scaffold, and a picture was taken. The test was repeated three times, and the average contact angle measurement was calculated.

The scaffolds are cut into circles of 1 cm diameter. Each piece was placed in a well of the plate and sterilized for 40 min under UV irradiation. Then, each well was filled with 96% alcohol, incubated for 3 h, taken out from the incubator, residual alcohol drained, and finally washed with sterile FBS. The moist scaffold containing 500 μl of media with 5% FBS was incubated in standard condition (37 °C with 5% CO2 and 95% humidity) for 24 h. After that, cells were seeded on every scaffold (8 μl of medium with about 1 × 104 cells). The cell-containing scaffolds were incubated at mentioned standard settings for 2 h for allowing cells to attach.

The cells proliferated, and the viability was monitored via MTT test. Scaffold-free cells were selected as the control agent. Cells that seeded on the PCL and CD/PCL scaffolds were monitored for 3 days. After washing two times by PBS, MTT solution was added to the specimens, incubated at 37 °C and 5% CO2 atmosphere for 3 h, and the test was followed by the addition of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) as a solvent. A better dissolution was obtained by placing the plate on a shaker. A microplate reader (CLARIOStar, BMG Germany) was used to record absorbance at 570 nm. Assay of MTT tests was performed three times.

Nucleus of live cells that grow within the PCL and CD/PCL scaffolds stained using DAPI tags. After 24 h incubation, the culture media was removed, and the scaffolds were washed three times by PBS solution. The remaining cells were fixed on the scaffolds with 4% paraformaldehyde for 25 min at 4 °C. Then, exposed to ambient temperature for 5 min and washed with PBS two times. In the end, 10 μL of DAPI solution was poured into the scaffolds and incubated for 1 min. The nuclei of cells were imaged by fluorescent microscopes (Euromex Oxion, Netherlands).

The attachment level and morphology of growth cells evaluated by SEM. Cell-containing scaffolds (PCL, CD/PCL) were cultured for 3 days and then washed with PBS. After immediate fixation in a 2.5% glutaraldehyde solution, cells were subjected to dehydration by a gradient of ethanol (60, 70, 80, 90, and 96%), and the critical point drying was done under a laminar flow cabinet. An SEM was applied to view gold-coated samples.

The optical properties of synthesized CDs were investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. In the UV–vis curve, CDs show absorption peak at 271 nm relates to π→π∗ transition of the aromatic (CC) sp2 hybrid orbitals. Another shoulder exposed in 325 nm created by n→π∗ transition band of the (C= O) sp3 hybrid orbitals (Fig. 2 a) [28]. Fluorescence emission spectrum obtained by exciting of CDs in 365 nm, and the highest emission observed at 430 nm (Fig. 2b). The fluorescent QY of CDs was calculated as 7.2% using quinine sulfate (quantum yield 54.6%) as a reference. Table 1 compared QY of synthesized CDs with other similar reports of CDs derived from the different eco-friendly precursors.

When the excitation wavelength changes from 350 to 530 nm (Fig. 2c), the maximum emission wavelengths gradually red-shifted. This shows the excitation-dependent emission property of CDs, which correlated to the existence of different energy levels and the numerous functional groups on the surface of CDs. The size distribution of prepared CDs is shown in Fig. 2d. The particle size distribution was obtained at about 4 nm. The TEM images of spherical, properly distributed CDs are shown in Fig. 2e, which confirms the result of DLS. Raman spectrum (Fig. 2f) was drowning to investigate the levels of CDs graphitization. The peak at 1372 cm−1, known as D-band, indicates defective/disordered crystallites of graphite. Another peak at 1597 cm−1 shows the G-band of graphite related to the CC in-plane stretching vibration (E2g) of graphite. D to G band ratio (ID/IG) represents the level of defects in the graphitic structure of CDs. Hence, the lower value of ID/IG in CDs indicates lower corrosion and oxidation reactions and so, the higher integrity of the graphitic shells, and finally, more graphitization. The ID/IG ratio of the CDs is ~0.68, which reveals the appropriate level of sp2 band formation in comparison with structural defects [29]. The main characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 2g) are observable at 3360, 2925, 2850, 1704, 1623, 1455, and 1029 cm−1, which are related to the energy transfer of stretching movements in O–H, -C–H, CO, CC, CN, and –C–O bands, respectively that indicate the high amount of oxygenated functional groups in these CDs [32]. The elemental analysis results confirm the existence of C, O, N, H, and S elements in the obtained CDs and their percentage 55.91%, 32.22%, 3.39%, 7.77%, and 0.71%, respectively.

The morphology of developed scaffolds studied by SEM imaging. Fig. 3 a and b show the homogenous structure of PCL scaffolds with no beads and deformity. Fig. 3c and d show SEM images of CD/PCL scaffolds. No beads and agglomeration were observed, which indicates CDs are well dispersed in the polymeric matrix. Furthermore, the incorporation of CDs in PCL scaffolds increases the median fiber diameter. Also, Nie et al. reported an enhancement in fiber diameter after the addition of CDs to the scaffold [36,37]. CD/PCL scaffold has improved tensile strength compared with CD-free scaffold. The CD/PCL scaffold showed breaking stress at 0.33 MPa and a breaking elongation of 306.39%, in contrast to 0.17 MPa and 101.03% for the PCL scaffolds. The addition of CDs to the spinning solution may be improved spinnability and increase the stretching of nanofibers during processing. Such mechanical improvement can relate to proper interfacial contact between CDs and the polymeric matrix. The presence of the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs can favor interfacial interactions within the polymeric matrix, resulting in better mechanical strength [38].

According to Table 2 results, the water contact angle of PCL and CD/PCL scaffolds were 84° and 0°, respectively. The estimated contact angle of the PCL scaffold (84°) comes from its hydrophobic nature. Accordingly, hydrophilic CDs have modified the wettability of PCL scaffolds and elevated their hydrophilic property. The hydrophilic surfaces of scaffolds allow the cells to attach, proliferate, and migrate appropriately [39].

Fig. 4 a shows that the UV–vis spectra of PBS/Scaffold are slightly different from PBS buffer (control). However, the characteristic peaks of CDs are not seen in this spectrum, so this difference is not related to the leak of CDs, or the amount of leakage was very small after 7 days. In addition, this difference may be due to the partial degradation of the scaffold in the PBS as its hydrophilicity has increased due to the presence of CDs [19].

In Fig. 4b, the mesenchymal cell viability in the presence of scaffolds (PCL, CD/PCL) was assessed by MTT assay. The polystyrene plate was considered as a control. CD/PCL scaffold shows higher cell viability due to its higher hydrophilicity. According to the diagram, the control sample had the lowest cell viability that maybe rise from its surface morphology. The plate has a completely smooth and two-dimensional surface. In contrast, the surface of the scaffolding is rough and three-dimensional.

For validating the attachment and viability of mesenchymal cells on PCL and CD/PCL scaffolds, the DAPI staining method was utilized. The staining images of cell nuclei were shown in Fig. 5 . CD/PCL contains more cells, which shows the effect of CDs on increasing the cell-scaffold adhesion (Fig. 5b). According to these results, CDs are biocompatible and increase cell adhesion of scaffolds due to their hydrophilic properties.

The morphologic status of cells up to 3 days was illustrated in Fig. 6 . On Day 1, adhered cells were observed on the scaffolds in a spherical shape. The cell in the PCL scaffold has a few amounts of expansion and interaction, which is attributed to the hydrophobic nature of the scaffold. In contrast, the cells appropriately expand and interact with the CD/PCL scaffold with good morphology.

CDs are carbon-based nanomaterial with various applications due to their remarkable optical properties, biocompatibility, stability, low cost of synthesis, and low toxicity. In this study, blue-emissive and water-soluble CDs were prepared via a green, inexpensive, and hydrothermal approach from Nigella Sativa seeds. The CDs display maximum emission at 430 nm, quantum yield (~7%), and small size (~4 nm). Electrospinning technique utilized to obtain CD/PCL scaffolds. Our results showed that the addition of CDs to the PCL scaffolds could improve the wettability and mechanical properties of scaffolds. The results indicated that CDs could improve the hydrophilicity and biocompatibility of the scaffold. Moreover, CDs increase the growth of mesenchymal cells on the scaffolds, which confirms the potential of this scaffold in tissue engineering.
Hamide Ehtesabi: Conceptualization, Writing- Original draft, Writing - review & editing.
Fatemeh Massah: Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to those who supported them, especially the vice-presidency for research and technology, Shahid Beheshti University.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sustainable synthesis of carbon quantum dots from banana peel waste using hydrothermal process for in vivo bioimaging
Phys. E Low-dimens. Syst. Nanostruct.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots originating from unripe peach for fluorescent bioimaging and electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction
J. Colloid Interface Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Atchudan, R., et al., Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from Phyllanthus acidus utilized as a fluorescent probe for label-free selective detection of Fe3+ ions, live cell imaging and fluorescent ink. Biosensors and Bioelectronics, 2018. 99: p. 303-311.

J. Mol. Liq.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green synthesis of multi-color emissive carbon dots from Manilkara zapota fruits for bioimaging of bacterial and fungal cells
J. Photochem. Photobiol. B Biol.

Analyst
